purpose: Build a thin Eclipse plugin that acts as an IDE adapter for Ananta rather than duplicating orchestration inside the IDE.
goal: Eclipse users can start and inspect Ananta-controlled project work directly from the IDE using existing Ananta APIs and control-plane flows.
success_criteria: The plugin can authenticate against Ananta and store endpoint/profile settings safely.
Users can send file, selection and project context from Eclipse to Ananta.
Users can trigger core flows such as analyze, review, patch, new-project and evolve-project from the IDE.
Returned tasks, proposals, artifacts and statuses are visible inside Eclipse without rebuilding Ananta logic in the plugin.
The plugin stays thin and delegates planning, routing, review and governance to Ananta.
program_success_criteria: The plugin can authenticate against Ananta and store endpoint/profile settings safely.
Users can send file, selection and project context from Eclipse to Ananta.
Users can trigger core flows such as analyze, review, patch, new-project and evolve-project from the IDE.
Returned tasks, proposals, artifacts and statuses are visible inside Eclipse without rebuilding Ananta logic in the plugin.
The plugin stays thin and delegates planning, routing, review and governance to Ananta.
architecture_guardrails: The plugin is an adapter UI only; hub orchestration, governance and policy decisions stay in Ananta.
No duplicate worker orchestration or policy engine is introduced inside Eclipse.
Sensitive data handling remains explicit, bounded and auditable.

milestones.title: Core IDE workflows and safety baseline

tasks.title: Plugin target model define
acceptance_criteria: A design note explains that the Eclipse plugin is a thin IDE adapter and not a second control plane.
The document lists what stays in Eclipse and what stays in Ananta.
The design explicitly forbids duplicate orchestration logic inside the plugin.

tasks.title: Minimum supported Eclipse distribution define
acceptance_criteria: Supported Eclipse versions and Java baseline are documented.
Required target platform or plugin dependencies are listed.
A simple local dev setup for the plugin exists.

tasks.title: Connection profile and authentication support add
acceptance_criteria: Users can configure Ananta base URL and auth method.
The plugin can login via the canonical auth flow or use a token profile.
Sensitive values are stored safely and not dumped into logs.

tasks.title: Basic health and capability handshake implement
acceptance_criteria: The plugin can query health/ready or equivalent capability endpoints.
The plugin can detect whether the target Ananta instance is reachable and usable.
The UI shows a clear connected/disconnected state.

tasks.title: Workspace and project context collection implement
acceptance_criteria: The plugin can detect current workspace, project name and selected file.
Context collection is explicit and bounded.
Users can review what will be sent before requesting an operation.

tasks.title: Selection and current editor content handoff add
acceptance_criteria: The plugin can send current text selection or current file path/context to Ananta.
Operations can target either selection-level or file-level scope.
Large content is clipped or summarized safely before sending.

tasks.title: Thin command set for core Ananta flows add
acceptance_criteria: The plugin exposes commands for analyze, review, patch, new-project and evolve-project.
Commands map to existing Ananta goal/task flows rather than custom plugin behavior.
Commands are accessible from editor context menu or command palette equivalent.

tasks.title: Quick prompt / goal input panel implement
acceptance_criteria: A small input panel allows users to describe a goal from Eclipse.
The panel supports sending project-aware context to Ananta.
The panel favors the official product paths rather than ad hoc raw prompting.

tasks.title: Task and artifact view inside Eclipse implement
acceptance_criteria: Returned tasks and artifacts can be listed in a dedicated view.
The user can open task details and inspect summaries without leaving the IDE.
The view distinguishes status, review requirements and next steps.

tasks.title: Status polling or refresh flow for tasks add
acceptance_criteria: Users can refresh running task state from Eclipse.
The plugin shows whether work is pending, blocked, done or review-required.
Refresh behavior is lightweight and does not spam the backend.

tasks.title: Diff/review result rendering add
acceptance_criteria: Review or change proposals can be rendered readably inside Eclipse.
Users can inspect proposed changes with file/path references where available.
The plugin does not apply changes automatically by default.

tasks.title: Review and approval action support add
acceptance_criteria: Where Ananta requires review, the plugin can surface that state.
Users can approve/reject applicable review items from the IDE when policy allows.
Review actions remain explicit and auditable.

tasks.title: Open in browser / open in Ananta shortcuts add
acceptance_criteria: Users can open the corresponding task/goal/artifact in the Ananta web UI.
Cross-linking works from Eclipse to Ananta for deeper inspection.
The plugin stays thin by delegating richer visualization to the web UI when useful.

tasks.title: Operation presets matching official use cases add
acceptance_criteria: Plugin actions clearly reflect official Ananta product paths.
At minimum, repository understanding, change review, bugfix planning, new project and project evolution are supported.
Terminology remains aligned with README/use-case language.

tasks.title: Optional SGPT/CLI direct operation bridge add
acceptance_criteria: The plugin can optionally invoke a bounded SGPT/CLI-backed operation through Ananta.
This path remains secondary to task/goal-based flows.
The plugin does not assume direct shell privileges.

tasks.title: OpenAI-compatible fallback experiment evaluate
acceptance_criteria: A short evaluation checks whether Eclipse-side integrations could reuse Ananta's OpenAI-compatible endpoints.
The result documents whether this is worth keeping as a fallback path.
The fallback does not replace the main plugin-controlled task flows.

tasks.title: Optional MCP-based integration path evaluate
acceptance_criteria: A short evaluation checks whether an MCP-based Eclipse path is realistic.
The document compares MCP versus direct REST integration for Eclipse.
The result helps avoid unnecessary plugin complexity.

tasks.title: Bounded context packaging rules define
acceptance_criteria: The plugin defines how much editor/workspace content may be sent for one operation.
Large project state is not blindly dumped to the backend.
Users can see and control the packaged context.

tasks.title: Security and privacy guardrails for IDE handoff add
acceptance_criteria: The plugin warns before sending sensitive content when possible.
Tokens, secrets and unrelated files are not sent implicitly.
Logs redact sensitive configuration values.

tasks.title: Error handling and degraded mode implement
acceptance_criteria: The plugin handles auth failures, connection problems and backend errors cleanly.
The UI gives actionable error messages instead of generic failures.
Operations fail visibly rather than silently hanging.

tasks.title: Task/goal trace visibility expose lightly
acceptance_criteria: Advanced users can inspect trace_id or routing summary when needed.
Default views remain simple and not overloaded.
The feature supports debugging without turning Eclipse into an ops console.

tasks.title: First-run UX inside Eclipse define
acceptance_criteria: A new user can connect Eclipse to Ananta and run a first useful flow quickly.
The first-run path recommends one of the official standard use cases.
No deep manual setup is required beyond endpoint and auth.

tasks.title: Golden path demo for Eclipse plugin document
acceptance_criteria: There is one official reproducible demo from Eclipse to Ananta.
The demo covers selecting code, sending a goal and viewing returned tasks/artifacts.
Another developer can reproduce it from documentation.

tasks.title: Automated tests for plugin core flows add
acceptance_criteria: Core plugin services have basic automated tests.
At minimum, config/auth, context packaging and task response parsing are tested.
Tests protect the thin-adapter design from accidental scope creep.

tasks.title: Manual smoke checklist for real Eclipse use add
acceptance_criteria: A small manual checklist verifies connection, send-selection, analyze and review flows.
Checklist is short enough for release smoke testing.
Known failure cases are documented.

tasks.title: Future roadmap for richer IDE support prepare
acceptance_criteria: Later ideas such as inline suggestions, richer diff views or deeper task boards are documented separately.
These ideas are not required for the first useful plugin release.
The MVP remains intentionally small and maintainable.

execution_order_summary: Stage 2: Context, command and goal handoff baseline (ECL-T05..T08)
Stage 3: Task/artifact UX and safety controls (ECL-T09..T14, ECL-T18..T20)
Stage 4: Extension paths and observability (ECL-T15..T17, ECL-T21..T22)
Stage 5: Validation, demos and rollout docs (ECL-T23..T26)
summary_notes: The eclipse_plugin_adapter track is fully complete: all 26 tasks are done.
Coverage now includes fallback-path evaluations, context/security guardrails, degraded-mode handling, trace visibility, first-run UX and rollout artifacts.
Critical path and all milestones are complete with thin-adapter boundaries preserved.
